does: This paper introduces LRHP, a framework for learning structured representations of human preferences from preference pairs rather than reducing them to single numerical reward values. Instead of the standard reward modeling approach that collapses preference information into a scalar for RLHF, LRHP constructs richer, multidimensional representations that capture nuanced aspects of human preferences. The authors demonstrate the utility of these learned representations on two downstream applications: preference data selection—identifying high-quality preference pairs for training—and preference margin prediction—estimating the strength of preference relationships. The framework operates on labeled preference pairs without requiring explicit reward values, enabling a more detailed encoding of human judgment.
connects: This work builds on the established pipeline of preference-based learning from human feedback, which has become central to aligning large language models. It responds to limitations in reward modeling approaches that have dominated RLHF since Bradley-Terry preference models were adapted for neural reward functions. The paper extends research on representing and analyzing human preferences more deeply, moving beyond prior work that treated preferences as binary labels or scalar rewards. It connects to broader efforts in representation learning and structured prediction, applying these techniques to the preference learning domain. The focus on preference pair datasets acknowledges the success of recent methods like DPO and IPO that work directly with preference pairs while avoiding explicit reward model training.
enables: By decoupling preference representation from reward value prediction, this work opens new directions for analyzing what human preferences actually encode. Richer representations could enable better interpretability of preference data and reveal systematic biases or inconsistencies in human judgments. The framework could support improved data curation and quality assessment, allowing practitioners to identify which preference examples are most informative or problematic. Future work might leverage these representations for transfer learning across preference datasets, combining preferences from multiple sources more effectively. The structured representations could also facilitate hybrid approaches that combine preference learning with other alignment techniques, or enable fine-grained preference optimization beyond standard RLHF, potentially leading to more controllable and interpretable model alignment.
